updates.note: On May 21, 2026, the West Bengal government altered the controversial media gag order following public backlash and opposition protests. A revised memorandum, signed by Additional Chief Secretary Rajesh Pandey, narrowed the scope of the restriction to apply only to regular establishments and parastatals under direct state administrative control.

additionalNotes.label: Deep Dive
additionalNotes.text: For a factual, source-based deep-dive into Circular No. 139-CS/2026—including its complete legal background, political reactions, and comparisons with similar rules in other states—read our complete Factual Deep-Dive Article.

updates.note: On May 21, 2026, West Bengal Chief Secretary Manoj Agarwal issued a formal administrative circular completely prohibiting government officials, civil servants, and police officers from communicating any document or information to the media without prior official sanction. Invoking multiple service rules, including the All India Services (AIS) Conduct Rules, 1968, the circular bans employees from participating in radio or media programs, writing articles/letters for publications, or making adverse criticisms of state or central government policies.

additionalNotes.label: Deep Dive
additionalNotes.text: For a factual, source-based deep-dive into Circular No. 139-CS/2026—including its complete legal background, political reactions, and comparisons with similar rules in other states—read our complete Factual Deep-Dive Article.

additionalNotes.label: Criticism
additionalNotes.text: The circular drew strong opposition from political and civil society circles. TMC General Secretary Abhishek Banerjee termed it a "gag order" designed to enforce silence. Additionally, associations of school and university teachers strongly condemned the directive as "undemocratic" and an unprecedented "fatwa" aimed at stifling their voices, arguing that it violates the constitutional right to freedom of expression.
